disease.name: Rectal Prolapse
disease.note: Intussusception of the rectal wall through the anal orifice. Most common in elderly women (peak at age 70). Full-thickness prolapse involves all layers of the rectal wall; mucosal prolapse involves only mucosa. The key distinction on examination is concentric rings (full-thickness) vs radial grooves (mucosal). In Kenya, this is commonly associated with prolonged constipation and multiparity. Surgical approach depends on the patient's fitness — perineal approaches for the frail, abdominal for the fit.
pathophysiologyDetail: Rectal prolapse is best understood as a sliding hernia of the anterior rectal wall through a defect in the pelvic floor, with the deep pouch of Douglas (peritoneal sac) acting as the hernia sac and the anterior rectal wall as its posterior lining

The pelvic floor (levator ani complex) weakens with age, multiparity, and chronic straining — the puborectalis muscle becomes stretched and separated anteriorly, creating a diastasis that no longer supports the rectum
Loss of the normal posterior rectal attachment to the sacral concavity (due to stretching of Waldeyer's fascia and the lateral ligaments) allows the rectum to straighten and intussuscept
The intussusception begins 6-8 cm above the anal verge at the level of the peritoneal reflection, where the anterior rectal wall is least supported and most mobile
A deep pouch of Douglas (cul-de-sac of the peritoneum extending down to the pelvic floor) allows the small bowel to push the anterior rectal wall downward, promoting the herniation process
Repeated increases in intra-abdominal pressure (from constipation, childbirth, chronic cough) progressively worsen the intussusception, which advances distally with each episode

As the intussusception reaches the anal canal, it triggers the rectoanal inhibitory reflex, causing transient relaxation of the internal anal sphincter and allowing the prolapse to emerge through the anal orifice

Full-thickness prolapse involves all layers of the rectal wall (mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, and serosa) — the concentric rings visible on examination correspond to the successive layers telescoping through the anus

Mucosal prolapse involves only the mucosa and submucosa, usually from laxity of the submucosal attachment to the underlying muscle — it is often associated with hemorrhoids and rarely exceeds 2-3 cm in length

Chronic prolapse stretches the anal sphincters, both internal (smooth muscle, resting tone) and external (striated muscle, voluntary squeeze), leading to progressive fecal incontinence even after successful prolapse repair
The pudendal nerve is stretched by the chronic prolapse and repeated straining (pudendal neuropathy), further compromising sphincter function and pelvic floor coordination

Solitary rectal ulcer syndrome (SRUS) is associated with prolapse in 50-70% of cases — the anterior rectal mucosa becomes traumatized by the intussuscepting rectal wall against the anal sphincter, causing ischemia, ulceration, and bleeding
In women, rectal prolapse is frequently associated with other pelvic organ prolapse — cystocele (60%), enterocele (30%), and vaginal vault prolapse (20%) — reflecting a global pelvic floor failure

In men, rectal prolapse is less common (M:F ratio 1:6) and is more often associated with neurological conditions (spinal cord injury, cauda equina syndrome, multiple sclerosis) or psychiatric disorders with chronic straining
Laxative abuse and chronic diarrhea syndromes can paradoxically cause prolapse by reducing stool bulk, increasing the frequency of defecation, and weakening pelvic floor support over time

landmarkTrials.population: 293 patients with full-thickness rectal prolapse across 21 UK centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Abdominal vs perineal approaches for rectal prolapse repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Abdominal approaches: lower recurrence (9% vs 22% at 3 years, p=0.02). Perineal approaches: lower morbidity (15% vs 35%, p=0.001). Quality of life similar between groups.
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed the principle of matching approach to patient fitness: abdominal rectopexy for fit patients, perineal rectosigmoidectomy for frail patients. No single 'best' operation for all patients.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic Ventral Rectopexy Outcomes (D'Hoore et al.)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic ventral rectopexy using synthetic mesh
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: 3% at 5 years. Improvement in incontinence: 80%. Improvement in constipation: 60%. Mesh erosion: 1.5%. Mean operative time: 75 minutes.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established laparoscopic ventral rectopexy (LVR) as the gold standard abdominal approach. Low recurrence, low morbidity, functional improvement in both incontinence and constipation.

landmarkTrials.trial: Altemeier vs Delorme for Full-Thickness Prolapse
landmarkTrials.population: 89 elderly patients with full-thickness rectal prolapse
landmarkTrials.intervention: Altemeier (perineal rectosigmoidectomy) vs Delorme (mucosal sleeve resection)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence at 3 years: Altemeier 16% vs Delorme 28% (p=0.04). Morbidity: Altemeier 12% vs Delorme 8% (p=0.52). Incontinence improvement similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Altemeier is superior to Delorme for full-thickness prolapse with lower recurrence rates. Delorme reserved for short segment prolapse or mucosal prolapse.

landmarkTrials.trial: Mesh vs Suture Rectopexy (Gala et al.)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: mesh 4% vs suture 8% at 2 years (p=0.18). Mesh erosion rate: 2% (synthetic mesh). Functional outcomes similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Suture rectopexy avoids mesh complications with only slightly higher recurrence. Mesh offers marginal benefit but carries rare but serious complications (erosion, infection). Choice depends on surgeon preference and patient factors.

landmarkTrials.population: 92 patients with full-thickness prolapse and constipation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Constipation improvement: 85% resection group vs 60% rectopexy alone (p=0.02). Recurrence: 4% vs 5% (ns). Anastomotic leak: 3% in resection group.
landmarkTrials.impact: Resection rectopexy is the procedure of choice for patients with significant constipation. The additional colonic resection addresses the underlying slow transit without compromising prolapse repair.

landmarkTrials.trial: Pelvic Floor Physiotherapy for Rectal Prolapse (PETFIT Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients with full-thickness prolapse awaiting surgery
landmarkTrials.intervention: Preoperative pelvic floor physiotherapy vs standard care
landmarkTrials.outcome: Postoperative incontinence scores better in physiotherapy group (p=0.03). Quality of life improved. No difference in recurrence.
landmarkTrials.impact: Preoperative pelvic floor physiotherapy improves postoperative continence outcomes. Should be offered to all patients undergoing prolapse repair where feasible.

evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard clinical test — ask patient to strain as if defecating in left lateral or squatting position. Full-thickness shows concentric rings; mucosal shows radial grooves.
Dynamic imaging — demonstrates intussusception, full-thickness vs mucosal, and associated pelvic floor abnormalities (rectocele, enterocele, cystocele).
Static and dynamic assessment of pelvic floor anatomy, levator ani integrity, and associated organ prolapse. Useful for preoperative planning.
Quantifies sphincter function — resting pressure (internal sphincter), squeeze pressure (external sphincter), and rectoanal inhibitory reflex. Predicts postoperative continence outcomes.
Essential to exclude: solitary rectal ulcer syndrome, proctitis, polyps, colorectal cancer. Also assesses the degree of rectosigmoid redundancy.
Identifies slow transit constipation — 20 markers ingested, abdominal X-ray at day 5. >5 retained markers indicates slow transit. Guides need for concurrent resection rectopexy.
Assesses sphincter integrity — identifies defects from obstetric trauma or prior anal surgery. Useful when planning concurrent sphincter repair.
Validated questionnaire for incontinence assessment (gas, liquid, solid, pad use, lifestyle). Essential for baseline and postoperative comparison.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical History — Characterize the prolapse: when it protrudes, how far, whether it reduces spontaneously or manually, associated incontinence, constipation, and other pelvic floor symptoms. Duration and progression over time.

Step 2: Straining Examination — Ask the patient to strain in the left lateral position or on a commode (squatting position is more physiological). Full-thickness prolapse: concentric mucosal rings, 5-15 cm length. Mucosal prolapse: radial lines, <3 cm length. Hemorrhoids: separate cushions with intervening grooves.

Step 3: Digital Rectal Examination — Assess sphincter tone (resting and squeeze). Identify any rectal mass. Assess perineal descent (>3 cm below ischial spines on straining suggests pelvic floor weakness). Check for rectocele.

Step 4: Assess for Associated Pelvic Organ Prolapse — In women: perform a pelvic examination to identify cystocele (anterior vaginal wall), enterocele (apical), rectocele (posterior vaginal wall), or vaginal vault prolapse. Rectal prolapse rarely occurs in isolation.

Step 5: Colonic Evaluation — Colonoscopy for all patients to exclude solitary rectal ulcer syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, polyps, or colorectal cancer. In the Kenyan context, where colorectal cancer incidence is rising, this is especially important.

Step 6: Anorectal Physiology — Manometry and endoanal ultrasound if available. Quantify sphincter function to predict postoperative continence and identify occult sphincter defects (obstetric trauma).

Step 7: Dynamic Imaging — Defecating proctogram (MR preferred, fluoroscopic if MRI unavailable) to confirm the diagnosis, differentiate full-thickness from mucosal prolapse, and identify associated pelvic floor abnormalities.

Step 8: Assess for Constipation — Detailed bowel habit history. Colonic transit study if slow transit constipation suspected (marker study). Patients with slow transit constipation benefit from resection rectopexy.

Step 9: Patient Fitness Assessment — Determine operative risk (ASA grade, comorbidities, frailty). Fit patients → laparoscopic ventral rectopexy (lowest recurrence). Frail patients → Altemeier or Delorme perineal procedure (higher recurrence but lower morbidity).

Step 10: Multidisciplinary Planning — Involve gynaecologist if concomitant pelvic organ prolapse. Involve physiotherapist for pelvic floor rehabilitation. Treat constipation preoperatively. Plan concurrent procedures (rectopexy + sacral colpopexy) for global pelvic floor failure.

managementPearls.pearl: The most important clinical question is: 'Can you push it back in, and does it stay in?' If the prolapse stays after manual reduction, the patient can manage conservatively until surgery. If it falls out immediately, the prolapse is advanced and surgical repair is more urgent.

Laparoscopic ventral rectopexy (LVR) is the gold standard for fit patients with full-thickness rectal prolapse — it corrects the anterior rectal wall defect, preserves the lateral ligamentous support, and avoids posterior dissection (reducing the risk of de novo constipation). Recurrence rate is <5% at 5 years.
managementPearls.evidence: D'Hoore et al., Br J Surg 2008

managementPearls.pearl: In the Altemeier procedure, the key to reducing recurrence is resecting adequate bowel length — at least 15-20 cm of sigmoid colon should be resected. Inadequate resection leaves the deep pouch of Douglas uncorrected and is the leading cause of recurrence after perineal repair.
managementPearls.evidence: Altemeier et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1971

managementPearls.pearl: Incarcerated or strangulated rectal prolapse is a surgical emergency. Attempt gentle manual reduction with sedation and the Trendelenburg position. Apply sugar or hypertonic solution to the prolapsed mucosa to reduce edema. If reduction fails or there is gangrene, emergency Altemeier resection is indicated.

Postoperative incontinence improves in 60-80% of patients after successful prolapse repair, but it takes 3-6 months. Do not consider additional sphincter repair at the time of prolapse repair — most patients regain adequate function after the mechanical correction of the prolapse alone.
managementPearls.evidence: PROSPER Trial 2013; D'Hoore et al., Br J Surg 2008

managementPearls.pearl: Constipation in rectal prolapse patients requires careful preoperative evaluation. If the patient has slow transit constipation (confirmed by transit study), resection rectopexy (Frykman-Goldberg) is the procedure of choice. If constipation is due to outlet obstruction from the prolapse itself, simple rectopexy often cures it.

In Kenya, many elderly patients with rectal prolapse present late with large, chronic, and ulcerated prolapse. Manual reduction may be difficult due to edema and chronic fibrosis. In these cases, admit for bed rest, elevation, and warm compresses for 24-48 hours to reduce edema before elective surgery.

Pelvic floor physiotherapy (Kegel exercises, biofeedback) should be offered to all patients both before and after prolapse repair. Preoperative physiotherapy improves postoperative continence outcomes (PETFIT trial). In Kenya, simple written and pictorial instructions for Kegel exercises can be provided where formal physiotherapy is unavailable.

Mucosal prolapse in young patients should raise suspicion for underlying conditions: cystic fibrosis (children), Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, or chronic straining from eating disorders. In young women, examine for pelvic floor integrity — early intervention may prevent progression to full-thickness prolapse.

When performing perineal rectosigmoidectomy under local anaesthesia (for extremely high-risk patients), the key is generous infiltration of the perineum and mesorectum with 1% Lidocaine with Adrenaline. The adrenaline reduces bleeding from the mesenteric vessels and provides a hydrodissection plane. This technique is particularly useful in Kenyan district hospitals without general anaesthesia capabilities.
managementPearls.context: Perineal surgery under LA — resource-limited setting
managementPearls.evidence: Altemeier et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1971; local adaptation

operativeNuances.nuance: In laparoscopic ventral rectopexy, the critical step is identifying the right hypogastric nerve (presacral parasympathetic fibers) during the dissection over the sacral promontory. The nerve lies just beneath the peritoneum, anterior and lateral to the sacral promontory. Use blunt dissection and stay precisely in the midline avascular presacral plane to avoid nerve injury causing retrograde ejaculation in men and urinary dysfunction.
operativeNuances.evidence: D'Hoore et al., Br J Surg 2008

operativeNuances.nuance: For the Altemeier procedure, after completing the perineal resection, ensure the anastomosis is performed at the dentate line — not above it. An anastomosis placed too high leaves behind a rectal stump that can act as a lead point for recurrent prolapse. A single layer of interrupted 3-0 absorbable sutures is sufficient.
operativeNuances.evidence: Altemeier et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1971

operativeNuances.nuance: In the Delorme procedure, the mucosal sleeve dissection should include the entire prolapse segment plus an additional 2-3 cm of normal mucosa above the levator ani. The muscle plication must be done with interrupted absorbable sutures that incorporate the underlying muscle of each quadrant. Plication too loosely → recurrence. Plication too tightly → outlet obstruction.
operativeNuances.evidence: Delorme et al., Bull Mem Soc Chir Paris 1900; modern series

operativeNuances.nuance: During laparoscopic ventral rectopexy, the mesh should be fixed to the anterior and lateral aspects of the rectum (not the posterior rectum) at 3-4 cm above the anal verge (level of the pelvic floor). Sutures placed too low → rectal pain and urgency. Placed too high → inadequate support of the prolapse segment.

For combined rectal prolapse and symptomatic enterocele/cystocele, perform laparoscopic ventral rectopexy with a second strip of mesh for the anterior compartment (sacral colpopexy). The same mesh strip can be split into two tails — one fixed to the rectum (posterior), one to the vaginal vault (anterior). This addresses global pelvic floor failure without additional ports.
operativeNuances.evidence: Slawik et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2008

operativeNuances.nuance: In the rare case of strangulated rectal prolapse requiring emergency resection, a perineal approach (Altemeier) is safer than a laparotomy in the septic patient. If there is gangrene of the prolapsed segment, resect the gangrenous bowel transanally and create a terminal colostomy (perineal colostomy) rather than attempting an anastomosis in an ischemic field.
operativeNuances.evidence: Ramanujam et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1994

prognosis.naturalHistory: Rectal prolapse is a progressive condition — without treatment, the prolapse becomes longer, more frequent, and more difficult to reduce. The anal sphincter progressively stretches, worsening incontinence. Approximately 10-15% of untreated prolapse eventually incarcerates or strangulates. Pelvic organ prolapse in other compartments (cystocele, enterocele) progresses concurrently.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After laparoscopic ventral rectopexy: 3-8% at 5 years. After resection rectopexy (Frykman-Goldberg): 4-6% at 5 years. After perineal rectosigmoidectomy (Altemeier): 10-20% at 5 years. After Delorme procedure: 15-30% at 5 years. Most recurrences occur within the first 2 years. Recurrence is higher with longer prolapse, larger prolapse, persistent constipation, and obesity.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Rectal prolapse itself does not increase colorectal cancer risk. However, the chronic mucosal trauma and ulceration of solitary rectal ulcer syndrome (associated with prolapse) can mimic malignancy on endoscopy. Biopsy is essential to differentiate. Any suspicious mass in a prolapsing rectum should be biopsied — case reports of adenocarcinoma arising in chronic prolapse exist.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Rectal prolapse severely impairs quality of life through the combination of the visible lump, fecal incontinence, mucous discharge, bleeding, and social embarrassment. Many elderly patients become housebound or institutionalized due to the condition. Successful surgical repair dramatically improves quality of life scores, with 80-90% of patients reporting satisfaction with surgical outcome.
prognosis.mortality: Elective rectal prolapse repair carries very low mortality (<1%). Emergency surgery for strangulated prolapse carries 5-10% mortality (due to sepsis, bowel ischemia, and patient comorbidities). In frail elderly patients, the risks of general anaesthesia and abdominal surgery must be weighed against the functional benefits of prolapse correction.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse outcomes: age >80, ASA III-IV, obesity (BMI >35), chronic constipation, chronic cough (COPD), heavy lifting occupations, neurological disorders, and previous failed prolapse repair. Protective factors: pelvic floor physiotherapy, normal BMI, treatment of constipation, avoidance of straining, and timely surgical intervention before significant sphincter damage occurs.

presentingComplaints: A mass protruding from the anus during bowel movements or even when walking
Difficulty controlling gas and stool (fecal incontinence)
Mucous discharge and bleeding from the prolapsed tissue
Sensation of a bulge or incomplete evacuation after bowel movements
Constipation with a need for manual assistance to pass stool

history_questions.question: When did you first notice a lump coming out of the back passage?
Does it come out only with bowel movements, or also with walking or standing?
Can you push the lump back in yourself? Does it stay in or fall back out?
Do you have leakage of stool or gas that you cannot control?
How long have you had difficulty controlling your bowels?
Do you have a history of prolonged constipation with straining?
How many children have you given birth to? Were they normal delivery?
Do you have a sensation that your bowel does not empty completely?
Do you have to put fingers in your vagina or anus to help pass stool (splinting)?
Do you have any bleeding from the prolapsed lump?
Do you have any mucous discharge?
Have you had any previous pelvic surgeries (hysterectomy, prolapse repair)?
Do you have a sensation of a bulge in the vagina or a dragging feeling?
Do you have any lower back pain or pelvic pressure?
Have you been diagnosed with any neurological condition?
Do you have any history of chronic cough or heavy lifting?
Have you tried any treatments or surgeries for this before?
How has this condition affected your daily life and activities?
Do you wear pads because of the discharge or incontinence?
Is the prolapse getting longer or more frequent over time?
Have you ever had treatment or surgery for prolapse or pelvic floor problems before?
Do you have diabetes or any condition that affects the nerves in your legs or back?
What do you eat in a typical day? Do you feel your diet contributes to constipation?
Do you use traditional remedies or herbs to treat the prolapse?

examination_findings.finding: Full-thickness rectal prolapse on straining — all layers protruding with concentric mucosal rings
Mucosal prolapse — radial grooves, only mucosa protruding
Patulous anus (gaping) with decreased resting sphincter tone
Sphincter tone decreased on voluntary contraction (squeeze pressure)
Concentric rings of mucosa distinguishing full-thickness from hemorrhoidal cushions
Ulceration or bleeding from the exposed prolapsed mucosa
Anterior rectocele or enterocele on pelvic examination
Cystocele or vaginal vault prolapse (associated pelvic organ prolapse)
Strangulated prolapse — dusky or black mucosa, gangrenous
Perineal descent on Valsalva (>3cm below ischial spines)
Anal reflex absent in neurological causes
Solitary rectal ulcer on proctoscopy — anterior wall ulceration at the intussusception site
Concomitant cystocele — anterior vaginal wall bulging on straining
Enterocele — bulging of the posterior vaginal fornix with small bowel contents
Perineal body deficiency — thin or absent tissue between anus and vagina

bedside_scores.name: Oxford Prolapse Grade

components.name: Grade I — Prolapse on straining, visible at anal verge
Grade II — Prolapse through anal canal on straining, reduces spontaneously
Grade III — Prolapse requiring manual reduction

bedside_scores.interpretation: Grade I-II: perineal approaches possible. Grade III-V: abdominal approach preferred for fit patients; Altemeier perineal rectosigmoidectomy for frail patients.

subtypes.name: Mucosal Prolapse
subtypes.management: High fiber diet. Stool softeners. Injection sclerotherapy. Mucosal banding. Delorme procedure for short segment mucosal prolapse.

subtypes.name: Full-Thickness Rectal Prolapse
subtypes.management: Perineal approaches (for elderly/frail): Delorme procedure (mucosal sleeve resection + muscle plication), Altemeier perineal rectosigmoidectomy (full-thickness resection + anastomosis). Abdominal approaches (for fit patients): Laparoscopic ventral rectopexy (LVR) — gold standard, Resection rectopexy (Frykman-Goldberg), Sacral colpopexy if combined vaginal prolapse.

subtypes.name: Incarcerated Rectal Prolapse
subtypes.management: Attempt gentle manual reduction with sedation and Trendelenburg position. Apply sugar granules to mucosa to reduce edema osmotically. Warm compresses. If reduction successful: elective repair in 1-2 weeks. If unsuccessful: emergency surgery — Altemeier procedure. In frail patients with irreducible viable prolapse, conservative management with regular manual reduction attempted by patient may be acceptable.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Rectal Prolapse
subtypes.management: Emergency perineal rectosigmoidectomy (Altemeier). If gangrenous segment is extensive or there is peritonitis: resect with colostomy (perineal colostomy). IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. Fluid resuscitation. ICU postoperatively. Do NOT attempt manual reduction of gangrenous bowel — risk of perforation and fecal peritonitis.

bedside.name: Straining examination in squatting or left lateral position
bedside.indication: ask patient to strain as if defecating to reproduce prolapse

bedside.name: Digital rectal examination
bedside.indication: assess sphincter tone, rule out rectal mass

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: rarely indicated unless bleeding or anemia

imaging.name: Defecating Proctogram (MR or fluoroscopic)
imaging.indication: dynamic assessment of pelvic floor, full-thickness vs mucosal, associated organ prolapse

imaging.name: Pelvic MRI
imaging.indication: assess pelvic floor anatomy, levator ani integrity

imaging.name: Colonoscopy
imaging.indication: rule out colonic pathology, solitary rectal ulcer syndrome, colitis

imaging.name: Anorectal Manometry
imaging.indication: assess sphincter function — helps predict postop continence

imaging.name: Transit marker study
imaging.indication: if severe constipation to assess colonic transit time (slow transit constipation)

interpretation_rules.concentric_vs_radial: Full-thickness prolapse shows concentric mucosal rings (like a toilet paper roll). Mucosal prolapse shows radial lines (like a star). Hemorrhoids show cushion-like separations.
interpretation_rules.preop_manometry: Low resting pressure suggests sphincter damage. Postop improvement is common after prolapse repair. High-grade incontinence may require concurrent or subsequent sphincter repair.
interpretation_rules.choose_approach_by_fitness: Perineal approaches (Altemeier/Delorme) have higher recurrence (10-20%) but lower morbidity. Abdominal approaches (rectopexy) have lower recurrence (2-5%) but higher morbidity. Fit patients: abdominal. Frail: perineal.
interpretation_rules.constipation_workup: If severe constipation is present, treat it before surgery. Some patients need resection rectopexy (Frykman-Goldberg) to address both prolapse and constipation.

management.protocolReference: ASCRS Practice Parameters for Rectal Prolapse (2022)
management.initial: Straining avoidance. Stool softeners (high fiber, increased fluids). Manual reduction of prolapse when it occurs. Treat constipation aggressively before any surgical intervention.
management.definitive: Fit patients (younger, good functional status): Laparoscopic ventral rectopexy (LVR) — gold standard with low recurrence. Resection rectopexy (Frykman-Goldberg) if slow transit constipation. Frail patients (elderly, comorbidities): Altemeier perineal rectosigmoidectomy (resection + anastomosis) — better than Delorme for full-thickness. Delorme procedure (mucosal sleeve resection) for short segment or mucosal prolapse. Sacral colpopexy if concomitant vaginal prolapse.
management.postop: Avoid straining. Stool softeners for 4-6 weeks. No heavy lifting for 6 weeks. Encourage pelvic floor physiotherapy. Monitor for recurrence. Incontinence may improve over 3-6 months after successful repair.

pathways.criteria: limited mucosal protrusion, no full thickness, Grade I Oxford
pathways.treatment: Delorme procedure. Injection sclerotherapy. Sitz baths. Stool softeners. Avoid straining.

pathways.criteria: full thickness prolapse, medically fit for abdominal surgery
pathways.treatment: Laparoscopic ventral rectopexy (LVR) — preferred. Resection rectopexy (Frykman-Goldberg) if slow transit constipation. Sacral colpopexy if concomitant vaginal prolapse.

pathways.criteria: full thickness prolapse, >70 years, multiple comorbidities
pathways.treatment: Altemeier perineal rectosigmoidectomy — lower recurrence than Delorme for full-thickness. Delorme for short segments (<5cm).

pathways.criteria: irreducible prolapse with edema, ischemia, or gangrene
pathways.treatment: Emergency surgery. Altemeier procedure with resection. If gangrenous: resection with colostomy. IV antibiotics. ICU if septic.

operative_templates.indication: Full-thickness rectal prolapse in a medically fit patient
operative_templates.incision: 3-4 laparoscopic ports (umbilical camera, right iliac fossa, right upper quadrant, left iliac fossa)
steps: Position with steep Trendelenburg, catheterize
Diagnostic laparoscopy, identify sacral promontory
Open peritoneum over the sacral promontory
Dissect the presacral space (avoid hypogastric nerves!)
Open peritoneum of the rectovaginal/rectovesical pouch on the right side
Dissect the rectovaginal septum down to the pelvic floor (anterior to rectum)
Cut a strip of synthetic mesh (polypropylene or biological) ~3x15cm
Suture the mesh to the anterior and lateral aspects of the lower rectum (2-3 Ethibond sutures)
Stretch the mesh without tension to lift the rectum
Fix the mesh to the sacral promontory with tackers or sutures
Close the peritoneum over the mesh
Check haemostasis

operative_templates.indication: Full-thickness rectal prolapse in elderly/frail patients
operative_templates.incision: Circumferential incision through full thickness of prolapsed rectum 1-2cm above the dentate line
steps: Position in lithotomy, gentle prolapse of the rectum
Identify the dentate line
Infiltrate with adrenaline/saline solution for hydrodissection
Make a circumferential full-thickness incision through the outer rectal wall 1-2cm above the dentate line
Deliver the inner rectum and sigmoid through the incision
Divide the mesorectum and mesosigmoid sequentially with ligation of vessels
Continue until the sigmoid colon is mobilized and there is no tension
Divide the full thickness of bowel at the chosen level
Perform a hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis (interrupted absorbable sutures)
Ensure the anastomosis sits above the dentate line
Return the anastomosis into the pelvis
Place a drain if indicated

patient_education.explanation: Rectal prolapse is a condition where the rectum (the last part of the large bowel) turns inside out and protrudes through the anus. It is most common in elderly women, especially those who have had multiple children and years of constipation with straining. In Kenya, this is often seen in grandmothers who have had many children and a lifetime of constipation. The prolapse tends to worsen over time if not treated. Surgery can fix it — the type of surgery depends on your age and overall health.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you have: the prolapse becomes stuck and you cannot push it back in (incarceration), the prolapsed tissue turns black or dark (gangrene), severe pain, fever, heavy bleeding, or inability to pass urine after surgery.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting (more than 5kg) and strenuous exercise for 6-8 weeks after surgery. Walking is encouraged and helps with recovery. Avoid prolonged standing or sitting. Do pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises as instructed. If your work involves farming or lifting, you will need 6-8 weeks off.
patient_education.diet: The key to preventing recurrence is to avoid constipation and straining. Eat a high-fiber diet: fruits (mangoes, pawpaw, oranges), vegetables (sukuma wiki, cabbage, carrots), whole grains. Drink at least 8-10 glasses of water daily. Take stool softeners (Lactulose) as prescribed. Avoid straining at stool — if you cannot pass stool easily, take more softeners rather than pushing hard.
patient_education.medication: Take prescribed pain medication as needed. Continue stool softeners for at least 4-6 weeks after surgery. If you had a mesh repair, there are no specific restrictions. Pelvic floor physiotherapy will be arranged. If you have incontinence, it may take 3-6 months to improve after the prolapse is repaired.
patient_education.followUp: You will be seen at 2 weeks for wound check, 6 weeks for recovery assessment, and 3-6 months to confirm no recurrence. Incontinence will be reassessed. If you had a perineal procedure (Altemeier), a colonoscopy may be recommended in the future.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure patient passing urine. Confirm stool softener regimen. Educate on avoiding straining. Demonstrate manual reduction of prolapse if not repaired yet.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check. Assess for urinary retention. Review stool softener compliance. Pelvic floor exercise instruction.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Wound inspection. Assess for recurrence. Discuss return to normal activity. Ensure constipation management plan in place.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery evaluation. Assess for recurrence and continence improvement. Discharge to primary care if asymptomatic.
after_discharge.3_months: Long-term follow-up to assess for recurrence. Continence reassessment. 6-month and 1-year surveillance for mesh patients.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: prolapse recurs and becomes irreducible, prolapse turns dusky/black, severe pain, fever, heavy bleeding, urinary retention, or worsening incontinence.
